questions.q: What is the primary role of an Ingress Controller in a Kubernetes cluster?
options: To act as a reverse proxy and load balancer routing external HTTP/HTTPS traffic to internal Services
questions.explanation: An Ingress Controller (e.g. NGINX, Traefik) is a pod running in the cluster that watches Ingress resources and acts as the actual L7 reverse proxy, routing external HTTP/HTTPS requests to the correct internal Services based on host and path rules.

questions.q: An Ingress resource is created with a TLS section referencing `secretName: myapp-tls-secret`. What type of Kubernetes Secret must exist with that name?
options: A Secret of type `Opaque` containing a base64 token
A Secret of type `kubernetes.io/tls` containing `tls.crt` and `tls.key` data
questions.explanation: TLS Ingress requires a Secret of type `kubernetes.io/tls` with two keys: `tls.crt` (the certificate) and `tls.key` (the private key). This can be created with: kubectl create secret tls myapp-tls-secret --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key

questions.q: What is the difference between path-based and host-based Ingress routing?
options: Path-based routing uses the URL path (/api, /web) to select a backend; host-based routing uses the HTTP Host header (api.myapp.com, web.myapp.com) to select a backend.
Host-based routing uses iptables rules; path-based routing uses DNS records.
Path-based routing requires TLS; host-based routing works only over HTTP.
They are functionally identical — only the annotation syntax differs.
questions.explanation: Path-based routing inspects the URL path of incoming requests to route to different backends (e.g. /api → api-service). Host-based routing inspects the HTTP Host header to route requests for different domains (e.g. api.myapp.com vs web.myapp.com) to different backends. Both can be combined in a single Ingress resource.
